key_points: Nationalism in India was shaped by the anti-colonial movement, where diverse groups bonded over their shared struggle against British rule.
Mahatma Gandhi returned to India in 1915 and introduced the concept of Satyagraha, emphasizing the power of truth and non-violence.
The Rowlatt Act (1919) allowed the detention of political prisoners without trial, leading to widespread protests and the tragic Jallianwala Bagh massacre.
The Non-Cooperation-Khomafat Movement was launched to unite Hindus and Muslims, encouraging the boycott of British goods, schools, and courts.
The Salt March (1930) marked the beginning of the Civil Disobedience Movement, as Gandhi broke the colonial salt law at Dandi.
The movement ended the 'ideal of unity' with the Poona Pact (1932), which addressed the demands of Depressed Classes for reserved seats.
examples: Early Satyagrahas: Champaran (Peasants), Kheda (Farmers), Ahmedabad (Mill workers).
Boycott Actions: Picketing liquor shops, burning foreign cloth, and students leaving government schools.
Mass Movements: Dandi March (Salt Satyagraha) and the Civil Disobedience Movement.
Icons of Unity: Abanindranath Tagore’s Bharat Mata and the use of the Swaraj Flag.
formulas: Civil Disobedience = Non-Cooperation + Violation of Colonial Laws
exam_tips: Identify the locations of important Congress sessions (Calcutta, Nagpur, Madras) on the map.
Differentiate between Non-Cooperation (refusing help) and Civil Disobedience (breaking laws).
Remember that the Chauri Chaura incident led to the withdrawal of the Non-Cooperation Movement in 1922.
Focus on why the Simon Commission (1928) was boycotted—it had no Indian members.
